text: A single incision was given in the 7th intercostal space between the anterior and midaxillary lines.
Arm 4 was cancelled and arms 3, 2, 1 were used.
Camera was placed in arm 3 and kept at the posterior most part of the incision.
We had a fenestrated bipolar grasper in the left hand and a long bipolar in the right hand or we can use a Maryland bipolar also.
Dissection begins in the fissure.
In the first step of the surgery, we dissect out the interlobar lymph nodes and then the assistant gets a small lymph node forceps from outside and takes the lymph node.
Then we isolate the lingular branch of the pulmonary artery and pass a loop around it.
Once the loop is passed, a 45 mm curved tip robotic stapler with a white reload is inserted and the lingular branch of the pulmonary artery is stapled.
So the artery has been stapled and we dissect out the remaining lymph nodes.
Once the artery has been stapled, we turn towards the vein and we dissect out the lingular branches of the left superior pulmonary vein and pass a vessel loop around it.
You can see the two sub-segmental branches of the lingular vein.
You can see the right-handed instrument acting almost like a right angled forceps.
That is the dexterity of the robot.
A vessel loop is again passed around the lingular branches of the pulmonary vein.
Passage of a loop is advantageous because it allows us to have a control
and also a retraction on the vascular or the bronchial structure that we are attempting to staple.
A curved tip wide reload is again used and the assistant uses a suction to retract the lung and the lingular branches of the pulmonary vein are stapled in the same way.
It is important to remove the loop out of the way
because it might accidentally get caught in the stapler fire.
Stapling should be done only after confirming that the tip of the stapler is visible.
After dividing the vane, we move to the lingular division of the bronchus.
You can see the lingular division of the bronchus has been isolated nicely and then we pass a loop around the lingular division of the bronchus.
I prefer to use a black reload for the bronchus.
We occlude the bronchus with the stapler, the clamping function on the stapler and confirm by asking the anesthetist to ventilate the left lung.
Sometimes passage of stapler needs to be facilitated by our assistant surgeon at the bedside so that the stapler moves freely inside.
Now that the stapler is around, we ask the anesthetist to ventilate.
When the anesthetist ventilates, you can see
The lower lobe is getting ventilated.
The upper trisegment is getting ventilated.
Only a small portion of the lung in the left upper lobe is not getting ventilated.

Then to demarcate the intersegmental plane for complete segmentectomy, we switch on firefly mode in the da Vinci console and ask anesthetist to inject ICG endocyanin green at a dose of 0.25 mg per kg into the peripheral vein.
The target segment which has already been isolated from the pulmonary vasculature shows no fluorescence.
The perfused ICG allowed the remainder of the lung to become fluorescent green, allowing us to demarcate the intersegmental plane, which is marked by energy device.
So the demarcation between the lingula and the upper trisegment is seen clearly.
So it is being marked.
Thank you.
Once the marking has been done, a series of staplers is then applied and the segmental division was completed.
Here we use 45 curve tip green and blue staplers.

Care must be taken to ensure that we don't involve the upper lobe pulmonary vein in this stapler and also we must ensure that the stapled part of the lingular division of the upper lobe bronchus should remain on the specimen side.
Otherwise, it will become a glorified wedge resection or an incomplete segmentectomy.
Thank you.
The robotic stapler gives us great degree of freedom because of the articulating ability inside the chest.
It is important to use 45 mm staplers because they have a greater degree of freedom owing to shorter length than the 60 mm staplers.
That is the last stapler being applied.
The specimen is then extracted through the utility incision using an endo bag.
We then proceed to dissect out the posterior hilar lymph nodes.
Once the posterior hilar is done
we dissect deep into the subcarinal space and also dissect out the subcarinal lymph nodes.
The robot offers us great vision and excellent maneuverability to dissect out lymph nodes deep in the subcarinal space.
We then inject local anesthetic with a scalp vein set
for all our interspaces.
We give a 2 ml of local anesthetic in each interspace.
